title: Data Ingestion Latency
spl:
(index=main OR index=security OR index=os OR index=windows) earliest=-15m
| eval latency=_indextime-_time
| stats avg(latency) as avg_latency, perc95(latency) as p95_latency by index, sourcetype
| where p95_latency > 300
| sort -p95_latency
description: High indexing latency (difference between event time and index time) means stale data for searches. Detection enables root cause analysis.
value: High indexing latency (difference between event time and index time) means stale data for searches. Detection enables root cause analysis.
implementation: Sample events periodically and calculate `_indextime` minus `_time`. Alert when p95 latency exceeds 5 minutes for critical sourcetypes. Investigate queue buildup, network latency, or time parsing issues.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: Monitoring Console.
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: Any index (sampling `_time` vs `_indextime`).
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
Sample events periodically and calculate `_indextime` minus `_time`. Alert when p95 latency exceeds 5 minutes for critical sourcetypes. Investigate queue buildup, network latency, or time parsing issues.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
(index=main OR index=security OR index=os OR index=windows) earliest=-15m
| eval latency=_indextime-_time
| stats avg(latency) as avg_latency, perc95(latency) as p95_latency by index, sourcetype
| where p95_latency > 300
| sort -p95_latency
```

Understanding this SPL

**Data Ingestion Latency** ‚Äî High indexing latency (difference between event time and index time) means stale data for searches. Detection enables root cause analysis.

Documented **Data sources**: Any index (sampling `_time` vs `_indextime`). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Monitoring Console. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: main, security, os, windows.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=main, index=security, index=os, index=windows‚Ä¶. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **latency** ‚Äî often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
‚Ä¢ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by index, sourcetype** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
‚Ä¢ Filters the current rows with `where p95_latency > 300` ‚Äî typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (sourcetypes with high latency), Line chart (latency trend), Bar chart (latency by sourcetype).
visualization: Table (sourcetypes with high latency), Line chart (latency trend), Bar chart (latency by sourcetype).
